summary: A 41-year-old man with chronic pancreatitis on long-term hydromorphone (~60 MME/day) is offered take-home naloxone by his primary clinician. He asks the office nurse why he needs it when he doesn't think he's at risk, and how to use it. The held-out answer must reflect FDA 2023 OTC approval of intranasal naloxone (Narcan), the CDC 2022 guideline recommendation for naloxone co-prescription at >=50 MME/day or with co-prescribed benzodiazepines or other risk factors, the practical use steps, the importance of Good Samaritan laws, and the post-administration plan (call 911 even after naloxone reverses overdose; recurrence is possible especially with long-acting opioids and fentanyl).

messages.content: I take hydromorphone for my chronic pancreatitis. My doctor handed me a Narcan prescription today and told me to keep it at home. I don't really get why — I'm not abusing this stuff. How am I supposed to even use it?

do_NOT: imply that being offered naloxone means the prescriber thinks he is misusing his medication
skip the recurrence-of-overdose warning after naloxone administration
describe naloxone as effective without the call-emergency-services caveat
ignore the OTC availability of intranasal naloxone

expected_sources_referenced: CDC Clinical Practice Guideline for Prescribing Opioids for Pain — Dowell et al., MMWR Recomm Rep 2022
FDA Approval of OTC Narcan Nasal Spray — March 2023 announcement
SAMHSA Opioid Overdose Prevention Toolkit — current edition
Good Samaritan overdose-prevention laws — state-level summaries (NLM / SAMHSA)
